content.text: Form, Change, Focus and Blur Events
Four event types drive most form interaction in React:
(form submitted). Each behaves slightly differently from its HTML counterpart — understanding those differences prevents the most common form bugs.

attrs.alt: Three panels: onChange (fires on every keystroke, different for text/checkbox/radio), onFocus/onBlur (element gains or loses focus; onFocusIn/Out bubble), onSubmit (on the form element, always call preventDefault)
attrs.title: Form, Change, Focus and Blur Events overview

content.text: This is the most important distinction to memorize. In HTML,
fires only when the element loses focus after the value has changed. In React,
fires on every keystroke, every character typed — closer to what the HTML
event does. This is intentional: it lets React keep state synchronized with the input on every change, which is the foundation of controlled components.
is the DOM element that fired the event. How you read the new value depends on the element type:
Or Number(e.target.value) — value is still a string
The value attribute of the selected radio
The value of the selected option
FileList — cannot control this input's value
These fire when the element gains or loses keyboard focus. They are used for:
Showing validation errors only after the user has left a field (not while typing).
Styling the active input (highlight border, show character counter).
Detecting when the user leaves an entire form section (using
Selecting all text in a focused input.
Focus Does Not Bubble — onFocusIn / onFocusOut Do
under the hood, so they DO bubble in React. This means you can detect focus entering any child by attaching
element, not to the submit button. This fires whenever the form is submitted — from a button click, pressing Enter in a single-line input, or calling
Without it the browser reloads the page (submitting to the form's action URL), which discards all React state.
Reading All Form Values with FormData
For forms with many fields, you don't have to maintain state for each field. Pass the native form element to
to read all values at once:
File inputs are always uncontrolled in React — you cannot set
on them because the browser controls which files are selected for security reasons. Read the files from

There are two models for form inputs in React:
. React is the single source of truth. The input always shows exactly what state holds.

Warning signs you've mixed up the models:

"A component is changing an uncontrolled input to be controlled" — state starts as
or is fetched asynchronously, then set to a string. Initialize to

triggers an expensive operation — a search API call, a filter over a large list — calling it on every keystroke creates too much work. The standard fix is debouncing: wait until the user has stopped typing for N milliseconds before running the expensive operation.
fires on every keystroke — not just on blur like the native HTML event. This makes it suitable for controlled components.
to trigger validation after the user leaves a field, not while they're still typing.
to detect when focus leaves a container.
collects all named field values without needing separate state for each input.
